news_summary: Recent advances in human rights highlight strengthened gender recognition for adolescents in Ecuador, grassroots feminist efforts promoting menstrual dignity and digital safety, and ongoing struggles to protect freedom of expression.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes en matière de droits humains mettent en évidence une meilleure reconnaissance du genre chez les adolescents en Équateur, les efforts féministes locaux visant à promouvoir la dignité menstruelle et la sécurité numérique, ainsi que les luttes continues pour protéger la liberté d'expression.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve el fortalecimiento del reconocimiento de género para los adolescentes en Ecuador, los esfuerzos feministas de base que promueven la dignidad menstrual y la seguridad digital, y las luchas en curso para proteger la libertad de expresión.

articles.title: Ecuador: Court Affirms Adolescents’ Right to Gender Recognition
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              A woman exits the Constitutional Court building, in Quito, Ecuador, February 7, 2024.
                    © 2024 Dolores Ochoa/AP Photo
          
    


  
(Washington, DC, March 12, 2026) – Ecuador’s Constitutional Court’s ruling, made public on March 10, 2026, that people under 18 cannot automatically be refused a request to modify their gender on identity documents is an important victory for the rights of transgender youth, Human Rights Watch said today. The ruling affirms that constitutional protection cannot rest on rigid assumptions about age while ignoring adolescents’ lived realities, evolving capacities, and right to be heard. The case arose after Ecuador’s civil registry denied a request by the parents of a 15-year-old to change the gender marker in his identity documents. The registry relied on article 94 of the Organic Law on Identity and Civil Data Management and, by extension, article 32 of its regulations, which require applicants to reach the age of majority for gender recognition. In Judgment 4-24-CN/26, the court held that applying that rule automatically is unconstitutional in a case like this one, where the adolescent had the support of his guardians and psychosocial evaluations showing sufficient maturity to make a free, informed, and voluntary decision regarding his gender identity.“This ruling makes clear that dignity and identity cannot be postponed until adulthood,” said Cristian González Cabrera, senior researcher at Human Rights Watch. “The court rightly recognized that transgender youth are rights holders with progressive autonomy, not mere objects of protection.”The court anchored its reasoning in the rights to identity and to the free development of personality, as well as the principles of the best interests of the child, progressive autonomy, and the right of adolescents to be heard. It stressed that gender identity is “an essential dimension” of those rights and that timely recognition of gender identity is closely linked to adolescents’ integral development.The judgment contains remarkably clear language in defense of trans youths’ rights, Human Rights Watch said. The court found that conditioning rectification of the gender marker on reaching adulthood can negatively affect adolescents. It also warned that the age requirement rests on “a static and homogenizing conception of adolescence,” one that assumes all adolescents lack the capacity to understand the consequences of their decisions.The court went further, recognizing the real harm caused by bureaucratic refusal to acknowledge a young person’s lived identity. It found that the restriction, when applied automatically, may “generate adverse effects on the emotional, social, and psychological well-being” of adolescents with the maturity to decide, and that it gives to the civil registry the power to deny “identity that has already been constructed, lived, and recognized in other spaces of social life.” At the same time, the court emphasized that it was not eliminating safeguards. Rather, it required a more tailored approach, which could include individualized assessment, psychosocial support, family accompaniment, and effective listening to the person concerned. The court made clear that “age cannot be required as the determining and sole criterion” for evaluating an adolescent’s capacity for understanding, discernment, or autonomy, and that less restrictive alternatives exist to protect adolescents without sacrificing their rights.International human rights standards increasingly recognize that requiring medical or psychological evaluations as a condition for legal gender recognition can violate the rights to privacy, autonomy, and self-determination, and many countries have moved toward procedures based primarily on a person’s self-declared gender identity.The ruling does not strike down the statutory provisions but will change their application in similar cases. The court ordered the civil registry and the Judiciary Council to broadly disseminate its ruling among registry officials and judges nationwide.The decision builds on a significant ruling made public in January 2025 in favor of a transgender student whose rights had been violated when their school refused to recognize their gender identity. The court held that the student’s rights to equality, free development of personality, education, and the best interests of the child had been violated, and ordered the education authorities to develop and disseminate a mandatory protocol on respecting the rights of lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender children in schools within six months. The protocol was required to include guidance on the use of a child’s chosen name, dress, and bathroom access consistent with their gender identity. Implementation of that decision has been uneven and controversial. Reporting in Ecuador has documented that the updated protocol issued in 2026 still leaves important questions unresolved, including guidance for evaluating a student’s evolving capacity and the lack of a clear procedure for resolving disagreements. That gap between formal legal recognition and practical implementation matters, Human Rights Watch said. In a 2024 report, Human Rights Watch research documented that sexual violence remains endemic in Ecuador’s education system and that protective measures have progressed too slowly and unevenly to keep all children safe. The researchers underscored the need for stronger institutions, clearer protocols, and effective follow-through. The ruling also comes at a critical moment for Ecuador’s democratic institutions. Human Rights Watch reported that the government organized demonstrations against the Constitutional Court and that senior officials called its judges “enemies of the people,” prompting concern from international human rights bodies about threats to judicial independence and security. Respect for the court’s authority is essential, particularly when it is protecting the rights of groups that often face discrimination and political hostility. “Ecuadorian authorities should carry out the gender recognition judgment promptly by issuing clear guidance to civil registry officials, training judges, and administrative personnel, and bringing all relevant legislation in line with the constitutional standards the court has articulated,” González said. “Officials should respect and faithfully carry out Constitutional Court rulings and refrain from attacks that undermine the court’s role in safeguarding human rights.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image A woman exits the Constitutional Court building, in Quito, Ecuador, February 7, 2024. © 2024 Dolores Ochoa/AP Photo

(Washington, DC, March 12, 2026) – Ecuador’s Constitutional Court’s ruling, made public on March 10, 2026, that people under 18 cannot automatically be refused a request to modify their gender on identity documents is an important victory for the rights of transgender youth, Human Rights Watch said today. The ruling affirms that constitutional protection cannot rest on rigid assumptions about age while ignoring adolescents’ lived realities, evolving capacities, and right to be heard.

The case arose after Ecuador’s civil registry denied a request by the parents of a 15-year-old to change the gender marker in his identity documents. The registry relied on article 94 of the Organic Law on Identity and Civil Data Management and, by extension, article 32 of its regulations, which require applicants to reach the age of majority for gender recognition. In Judgment 4-24-CN/26, the court held that applying that rule automatically is unconstitutional in a case like this one, where the adolescent had the support of his guardians and psychosocial evaluations showing sufficient maturity to make a free, informed, and voluntary decision regarding his gender identity.

“This ruling makes clear that dignity and identity cannot be postponed until adulthood,” said Cristian González Cabrera, senior researcher at Human Rights Watch. “The court rightly recognized that transgender youth are rights holders with progressive autonomy, not mere objects of protection.”

The court anchored its reasoning in the rights to identity and to the free development of personality, as well as the principles of the best interests of the child, progressive autonomy, and the right of adolescents to be heard. It stressed that gender identity is “an essential dimension” of those rights and that timely recognition of gender identity is closely linked to adolescents’ integral development.

The judgment contains remarkably clear language in defense of trans youths’ rights, Human Rights Watch said. The court found that conditioning rectification of the gender marker on reaching adulthood can negatively affect adolescents. It also warned that the age requirement rests on “a static and homogenizing conception of adolescence,” one that assumes all adolescents lack the capacity to understand the consequences of their decisions.

The court went further, recognizing the real harm caused by bureaucratic refusal to acknowledge a young person’s lived identity. It found that the restriction, when applied automatically, may “generate adverse effects on the emotional, social, and psychological well-being” of adolescents with the maturity to decide, and that it gives to the civil registry the power to deny “identity that has already been constructed, lived, and recognized in other spaces of social life.”

At the same time, the court emphasized that it was not eliminating safeguards. Rather, it required a more tailored approach, which could include individualized assessment, psychosocial support, family accompaniment, and effective listening to the person concerned. The court made clear that “age cannot be required as the determining and sole criterion” for evaluating an adolescent’s capacity for understanding, discernment, or autonomy, and that less restrictive alternatives exist to protect adolescents without sacrificing their rights.

International human rights standards increasingly recognize that requiring medical or psychological evaluations as a condition for legal gender recognition can violate the rights to privacy, autonomy, and self-determination, and many countries have moved toward procedures based primarily on a person’s self-declared gender identity.

The ruling does not strike down the statutory provisions but will change their application in similar cases. The court ordered the civil registry and the Judiciary Council to broadly disseminate its ruling among registry officials and judges nationwide.

The decision builds on a significant ruling made public in January 2025 in favor of a transgender student whose rights had been violated when their school refused to recognize their gender identity. The court held that the student’s rights to equality, free development of personality, education, and the best interests of the child had been violated, and ordered the education authorities to develop and disseminate a mandatory protocol on respecting the rights of lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender children in schools within six months. The protocol was required to include guidance on the use of a child’s chosen name, dress, and bathroom access consistent with their gender identity.

Implementation of that decision has been uneven and controversial. Reporting in Ecuador has documented that the updated protocol issued in 2026 still leaves important questions unresolved, including guidance for evaluating a student’s evolving capacity and the lack of a clear procedure for resolving disagreements.

That gap between formal legal recognition and practical implementation matters, Human Rights Watch said. In a 2024 report, Human Rights Watch research documented that sexual violence remains endemic in Ecuador’s education system and that protective measures have progressed too slowly and unevenly to keep all children safe. The researchers underscored the need for stronger institutions, clearer protocols, and effective follow-through.

The ruling also comes at a critical moment for Ecuador’s democratic institutions. Human Rights Watch reported that the government organized demonstrations against the Constitutional Court and that senior officials called its judges “enemies of the people,” prompting concern from international human rights bodies about threats to judicial independence and security. Respect for the court’s authority is essential, particularly when it is protecting the rights of groups that often face discrimination and political hostility.

“Ecuadorian authorities should carry out the gender recognition judgment promptly by issuing clear guidance to civil registry officials, training judges, and administrative personnel, and bringing all relevant legislation in line with the constitutional standards the court has articulated,” González said. “Officials should respect and faithfully carry out Constitutional Court rulings and refrain from attacks that undermine the court’s role in safeguarding human rights.”
articles.reasoning: The Ecuadorian Constitutional Court ruling affirms transgender adolescents' right to gender recognition on identity documents, recognizing their evolving capacities and autonomy. This legal decision has broad societal significance, advancing human rights protections for transgender youth, promoting dignity, and mandating changes in administrative practices nationwide. The article provides detailed context on the ruling's legal basis, its implications for youth rights, and the broader human rights environment in Ecuador.
articles.personality_title: Ecuador court supports transgender youths’ right to change gender on ID before 18
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Ecuador, the law previously required people to be adults (18 or older) to change the gender listed on their official identity documents. This rule affected transgender adolescents who wanted their documents to match their gender identity.

**What happened** – On March 10, 2026, Ecuador’s Constitutional Court ruled that automatically denying transgender people under 18 the right to change their gender on ID cards is unconstitutional. This decision came after a 15-year-old’s parents asked to update his gender marker, but the civil registry refused because of the age rule. The court said that if the adolescent shows enough maturity and has support from guardians, they should be allowed to make this change.

**Impact** – This ruling is important because it recognizes that young people can understand and decide about their gender identity before adulthood. It protects their dignity and emotional well-being by allowing legal recognition that matches their lived identity. The court also said officials must not rely only on age but consider each case individually, including support and evaluations. This sets a new standard in Ecuador for respecting transgender youths’ rights.

**What’s next step** – The court ordered the civil registry and judges to spread this decision widely and change how they handle these cases. Ecuador’s authorities are expected to train officials and update their procedures to respect the ruling. This may lead to clearer and more supportive processes for transgender adolescents across the country.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ecuador’s highest court has ruled that transgender adolescents with enough maturity can legally change their gender on identity documents before turning 18, promoting respect for their rights and identity.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Cour constitutionnelle d’Équateur reconnaît le droit des adolescents transgenres à changer leur genre sur leurs papiers d’identité avant 18 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Équateur, la loi exigeait auparavant que les personnes soient majeures (18 ans ou plus) pour changer le genre indiqué sur leurs documents officiels. Cette règle affectait les adolescents transgenres souhaitant que leurs documents correspondent à leur identité de genre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 mars 2026, la Cour constitutionnelle d’Équateur a jugé inconstitutionnel de refuser automatiquement aux personnes transgenres de moins de 18 ans le droit de modifier leur genre sur leurs papiers d’identité. Cette décision fait suite à une demande des parents d’un adolescent de 15 ans, rejetée par l’état civil en raison de la règle d’âge. La Cour a estimé que si l’adolescent montre une maturité suffisante et bénéficie du soutien de ses tuteurs, il doit pouvoir effectuer ce changement.

**Impact** – Cette décision est importante car elle reconnaît que les jeunes peuvent comprendre et décider de leur identité de genre avant leur majorité. Elle protège leur dignité et leur bien-être émotionnel en permettant une reconnaissance légale conforme à leur identité vécue. La Cour a aussi précisé que les autorités ne doivent pas se baser uniquement sur l’âge, mais examiner chaque cas individuellement, avec accompagnement et évaluations. Cela établit une nouvelle norme en Équateur pour le respect des droits des jeunes transgenres.

**Prochaine étape** – La Cour a ordonné à l’état civil et aux juges de diffuser largement cette décision et de modifier leurs pratiques. Les autorités équatoriennes doivent former leurs agents et mettre à jour leurs procédures pour appliquer ce jugement. Cela pourrait conduire à des processus plus clairs et bienveillants pour les adolescents transgenres dans tout le pays.

**Phrase clé** – La plus haute Cour d’Équateur a statué que les adolescents transgenres suffisamment matures peuvent légalement changer leur genre sur leurs papiers d’identité avant 18 ans, renforçant ainsi le respect de leurs droits et de leur identité.
articles.personality_title_es: Corte constitucional de Ecuador reconoce el derecho de adolescentes transgénero a cambiar su género en documentos antes de los 18 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Ecuador, la ley requería que las personas fueran adultas (18 años o más) para cambiar el género en sus documentos oficiales. Esta regla afectaba a adolescentes transgénero que querían que sus documentos reflejaran su identidad de género.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de marzo de 2026, la Corte Constitucional de Ecuador dictaminó que negar automáticamente a personas transgénero menores de 18 años el derecho a modificar su género en documentos de identidad es inconstitucional. Esta decisión ocurrió después de que los padres de un joven de 15 años solicitaran el cambio y la oficina civil lo rechazara por la edad. La Corte dijo que si el adolescente muestra madurez suficiente y tiene el apoyo de sus tutores, debe poder realizar este cambio.

**Impacto** – Este fallo es importante porque reconoce que los jóvenes pueden comprender y decidir sobre su identidad de género antes de la mayoría de edad. Protege su dignidad y bienestar emocional al permitir el reconocimiento legal que coincide con su identidad vivida. La Corte también indicó que los funcionarios no deben basarse solo en la edad, sino evaluar cada caso individualmente, con apoyo y evaluaciones. Esto establece un nuevo estándar en Ecuador para respetar los derechos de los jóvenes transgénero.

**Próximo paso** – La Corte ordenó al registro civil y a los jueces difundir ampliamente esta decisión y cambiar la forma en que manejan estos casos. Se espera que las autoridades ecuatorianas capaciten a los funcionarios y actualicen sus procedimientos para respetar el fallo. Esto podría llevar a procesos más claros y solidarios para adolescentes transgénero en todo el país.

**Frase clave** – La máxima corte de Ecuador decidió que adolescentes transgénero con suficiente madurez pueden cambiar legalmente su género en documentos de identidad antes de los 18 años, promoviendo el respeto a sus derechos e identidad.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of an open door shaped like a constitutional court building, glowing softly with warm light, through which a young, androgynous silhouette steps confidently forward, accompanied by two supportive, larger silhouettes symbolizing guardianship; the scene uses natural, muted earth tones and soft pastels to evoke dignity, growth, and the affirmation of identity.

articles.title: From menstrual dignity to digital safety: How grassroots feminists are redefining gender justice
articles.summary: What connects a pad box in a Nigerian school, a coalition meeting in Pakistan, and a digital security workshop in Paraguay? They each address a structural barrier that limits women’s participation.
articles.content:
By Clarisse Sih and Bibbi Abruzzini, Forus

In a secondary school in southern Nigeria, a teenage girl got her period during class hours. She had no sanitary pads. The teacher sent her home.

For Udoka Anita Ikebua, then a young graduate posted to the school for national service, that moment became a turning point. “In the 21st century,” she remembers thinking, “how can a girl still not have access to something so basic?”

This is how Ikebua’s initiative evolved into one of Nigeria’s most significant menstrual justice victories: in March 2025, Bauchi State passed the country’s first bill establishing free sanitary pad banks in public schools and correctional centers — a shift from charity to rights-based policy.

Across continents, similar transformations are unfolding. In Pakistan, feminist advocates are reframing social protection as democratic infrastructure. In Paraguay, digital rights activists are challenging the normalization of gender-based violence.

Their struggles may look different — period poverty, policy reform, digital abuse — but they are united by a deeper truth: gender justice today is inseparable from economic security, digital safety, and institutional accountability.

Nigeria: From pad donations to policy reform

Ikebua founded Project Pad A Girl after witnessing firsthand how menstruation interrupted girls’ education. Initially, the solution seemed simple: distribute sanitary pads. But she quickly realized the limits of charity. “If I give one pad today,” she explains, “what happens next month?”

Girls without access to sanitary products during their periods often resort to using tissue paper, rags, leaves, or simply staying home. Teachers would sometimes send students away if they stained their uniforms.

The breakthrough came with the creation of pad banks: permanent, school-based emergency supply boxes stocked with menstrual products each term. Girls who begin menstruating during school hours can discreetly access supplies from the counselor’s office and remain in class.

The impact has been immediate: improved attendance, reduced stigma, and a sense of security.

“The idea that if my period comes, I am safe — that changes everything,” Ikebua says.

Crucially, Project Pad A Girl intentionally includes boys in menstrual education sessions to dismantle shame. “As you are not ashamed of your body,” Ikebua tells male students, “you should not make a girl ashamed of hers.”

The Bauchi legislation expanded this model further, to include correctional facilities, where incarcerated women often rely on rags and face severe hygiene risks. By introducing reusable sanitary pads designed to last months, the initiative centers dignity and sustainability.

“It’s about being seen — even behind prison walls,” Ikebua explains.

Yet challenges remain. Menstrual products are subject to a value-added tax, the removal of which advocates are lobbying for, but progress is slow in a male-dominated legislature where, Ikebua admits, “sometimes when you take it to men, they don’t really understand.”

Pakistan: Social protection as feminist infrastructure

For Marium Amjad Khan, programme manager at the Pakistan Development Alliance, feminism is not an abstract theory. It began with witnessing girls’ futures decided before they had a chance to dream.

“Once you see inequality,” she says, “you cannot unsee it.”

Working at both grassroots and policy levels, Khan focuses on child marriage, domestic violence, sexual and reproductive health rights, and education governance. But increasingly, she emphasizes social protection — economic systems that determine whether women can withstand crises.

Without economic security, democratic participation is fragile. Women burdened by poverty, unpaid care work, or climate shocks are less able to organize, advocate, or run for office. “Laws are only beginning,” Amjad explains. “Real change happens when communities begin to believe that girls deserve childhood, that women deserve safety.”

Pakistan has passed important legislation in recent years, but implementation remains uneven. A central challenge, she notes, is awareness: many communities do not know their rights. Coalitions, therefore, are essential. The Pakistan Development Alliance brings together over 115 civil society organizations, amplifying collective voices in policy spaces. “When communities speak together, policymakers listen,” Khan posits. “They have to.”

In a context of a shrinking enabling environment for civil society, global feminist solidarity provides reinforcement.

“When space shrinks in one country, international networks remind us we are not alone.”

For Khan, feminist leadership is not about being the loudest voice; it is about creating space for other voices.

Paraguay: Digital violence is real

While menstrual dignity and social protection operate in physical spaces, in Paraguay, the struggle unfolds online.

TEDIC, a digital rights organization, leads the campaign, La Violencia Digital es Real (Digital Violence is Real), to challenge the idea that online abuse is somehow less serious than offline harm.

“A message on the internet can cost someone their job, their safety, their peace,” explains project lead Jazmín Ruiz Díaz.

For years, the internet was treated as neutral, a space that would naturally advance equality. Instead, inequalities have been amplified.

TEDIC’s emblematic case is that of Belén Whittingslow, a student who accused a powerful university professor of harassment via explicit messages. The case was dismissed as “courtship.” She was later criminalized and forced to seek refuge abroad. The professor faced no legal consequences.

The case illustrates the layered nature of digital violence, how power, institutions, and platforms intersect. Beyond individual attacks, activists faced coordinated online harassment that attempted to silence their advocacy and discredit their work. The responses from institutions and the limitations of platform moderation further complicated the situation, revealing how gaps in accountability and regulation can allow online abuse to persist. In this way, the experience highlights how digital violence is not only a personal attack but also a structural issue shaped by social power dynamics, institutional responses, and the governance of digital platforms.

Ruiz Díaz also notes that perpetrators are not only anonymous trolls. These actors can range from coordinated harassment networks and state-backed troll farms to the platforms themselves, where opaque moderation systems and algorithmic bias have been widely criticized. Investigations and media reports have documented cases where online attacks against women journalists, politicians, and activists are amplified by organized networks, while platform responses remain inconsistent.

Through its Free and Safe on the Internet initiative, TEDIC provides digital security training to activists, LGBTQ+ communities, and civil society groups. The work is slow, incremental — “a work of ants,” Ruiz Díaz says — but necessary.

As new technologies emerge, so do new forms of harm. Deepfakes, non-consensual image manipulation, surveillance technologies, and AI tools have intensified risks.

“Cyberspace is not separate from reality,” Ruiz Díaz emphasizes. “Violence online has real effects on bodies and minds.”

Addressing it requires coordinated action from governments, tech companies, and civil society. But it also requires community care.

“The exit is collective,” she says, “and joy is also a form of resistance.”

Redefining gender justice

What connects a pad box in a Nigerian school, a coalition meeting in Pakistan, and a digital security workshop in Paraguay? They each address a structural barrier that limits women’s participation in education, in governance, in digital public life.

Menstrual dignity affects school retention and long-term economic opportunity. Social protection shapes political agency. Digital safety determines who can speak freely.

These are not peripheral concerns. They are pillars of democracy.

From classrooms to courtrooms to cyberspace, grassroots feminists are redefining gender justice — not as symbolic recognition, but as systemic transformation.

And in doing so, they are reshaping the institutions that govern everyday life.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant grassroots feminist initiatives across multiple countries that address structural barriers limiting women's participation in education, governance, and digital spaces. These efforts have led to tangible policy changes like Nigeria's free sanitary pad banks in schools and correctional facilities, coalition-building for social protection in Pakistan, and digital safety training in Paraguay. The story highlights systemic transformation towards gender justice with broad societal impact and detailed context.
articles.personality_title: Grassroots feminists worldwide tackle barriers to gender justice in schools, policy, and online
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
In many parts of the world, women and girls face hidden challenges that stop them from fully taking part in education, politics, and online life. These problems include lack of access to menstrual products, weak social protections, and digital violence.

**What happened**
In Nigeria, Udoka Anita Ikebua started Project Pad A Girl after seeing a student sent home for not having sanitary pads. Her work helped Bauchi State pass a law to provide free sanitary pad banks in schools and prisons. In Pakistan, Marium Amjad Khan leads a coalition helping women understand their rights and pushing for better social protections to support women’s economic security and political participation. In Paraguay, the group TEDIC fights digital violence against women by training activists and pushing for stronger online safety rules.

**Impact**
These efforts go beyond quick fixes and focus on changing systems that keep women from equal participation. Nigeria’s pad banks help keep girls in school and reduce shame. Pakistan’s coalitions bring many voices together to influence laws and attitudes about women’s rights. Paraguay’s digital safety work reveals how online abuse harms real lives and shows the need for better rules and community support. Together, these examples show how gender justice depends on tackling many connected issues, not just one.

**What's next step**
Advocates in all three countries aim to expand their work: Nigeria is pushing to remove taxes on menstrual products, Pakistan is strengthening community awareness and policy enforcement, and Paraguay is working to improve digital laws and protect activists. These ongoing efforts aim to build fairer institutions that support women’s rights across society.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Grassroots feminist actions in Nigeria, Pakistan, and Paraguay are reshaping systems to remove barriers and promote true gender justice in schools, governance, and online spaces.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des féministes locales redéfinissent la justice de genre dans les écoles, les politiques et le numérique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Dans plusieurs pays, les femmes et les filles rencontrent des obstacles cachés qui les empêchent de participer pleinement à l’éducation, à la politique et à la vie en ligne. Ces problèmes incluent le manque d’accès aux protections menstruelles, des protections sociales faibles et la violence numérique.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Au Nigeria, Udoka Anita Ikebua a lancé Project Pad A Girl après avoir vu une élève renvoyée chez elle faute de protections hygiéniques. Son initiative a aidé l’État de Bauchi à adopter une loi pour fournir des banques de protections menstruelles gratuites dans les écoles et les prisons. Au Pakistan, Marium Amjad Khan dirige une coalition qui aide les femmes à connaître leurs droits et à améliorer les protections sociales pour soutenir leur sécurité économique et leur participation politique. Au Paraguay, l’organisation TEDIC lutte contre la violence numérique envers les femmes en formant des activistes et en poussant à renforcer les règles de sécurité en ligne.

**Impact**
Ces actions vont au-delà de solutions temporaires et visent à changer les systèmes qui empêchent l’égalité des femmes. Les banques de protections au Nigeria permettent aux filles de rester à l’école et réduisent la honte. Les coalitions pakistanaises unissent de nombreuses voix pour influencer les lois et les mentalités. Le travail sur la sécurité numérique au Paraguay montre comment les abus en ligne affectent la vie réelle et souligne le besoin de meilleures règles et d’un soutien communautaire. Ensemble, ces exemples montrent que la justice de genre dépend de la lutte contre plusieurs problèmes liés.

**Prochaine étape**
Les défenseurs dans les trois pays cherchent à étendre leur travail : le Nigeria veut supprimer les taxes sur les protections menstruelles, le Pakistan renforce la sensibilisation et l’application des lois, et le Paraguay améliore les lois numériques pour protéger les activistes. Ces efforts visent à construire des institutions plus justes soutenant les droits des femmes.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Des actions féministes locales au Nigeria, au Pakistan et au Paraguay transforment les systèmes pour lever les obstacles et promouvoir une vraie justice de genre dans les écoles, la gouvernance et le numérique.
articles.personality_title_es: Feministas locales transforman la justicia de género en escuelas, políticas y espacios digitales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
En muchos países, mujeres y niñas enfrentan desafíos que les impiden participar plenamente en la educación, la política y la vida en línea. Estos problemas incluyen la falta de acceso a productos menstruales, protecciones sociales débiles y violencia digital.

**Qué pasó**
En Nigeria, Udoka Anita Ikebua inició el Proyecto Pad A Girl tras ver a una estudiante enviada a casa por no tener toallas sanitarias. Su trabajo ayudó al estado de Bauchi a aprobar una ley para ofrecer bancos gratuitos de productos menstruales en escuelas y cárceles. En Pakistán, Marium Amjad Khan lidera una coalición que ayuda a mujeres a conocer sus derechos y promueve mejores protecciones sociales para apoyar su seguridad económica y participación política. En Paraguay, la organización TEDIC combate la violencia digital contra mujeres capacitando activistas y promoviendo reglas más fuertes para la seguridad en internet.

**Impacto**
Estos esfuerzos van más allá de soluciones rápidas y buscan cambiar sistemas que impiden la igualdad. Los bancos de toallas en Nigeria ayudan a que las niñas permanezcan en la escuela y reducen la vergüenza. Las coaliciones en Pakistán unen muchas voces para influir en leyes y actitudes sobre derechos de las mujeres. El trabajo en seguridad digital en Paraguay muestra cómo el abuso en línea afecta vidas reales y la necesidad de mejores reglas y apoyo comunitario. Juntos, estos casos demuestran que la justicia de género requiere abordar problemas conectados.

**Próximos pasos**
Los defensores en los tres países quieren ampliar su trabajo: Nigeria busca eliminar impuestos a productos menstruales, Pakistán fortalece la conciencia comunitaria y la aplicación de leyes, y Paraguay trabaja para mejorar las leyes digitales y proteger activistas. Estos esfuerzos buscan construir instituciones más justas que apoyen los derechos de las mujeres.

**Resumen en una frase**
Acciones feministas locales en Nigeria, Pakistán y Paraguay están transformando sistemas para eliminar barreras y promover justicia de género real en escuelas, gobernanza y espacios digitales.

articles.title: Novelist Dinaw Mengestu on the battle for freedom of expression
articles.summary: As he takes on the presidency of PEN America, Dinaw Mengestu explains why defending a plurality of voices is crucial at a time of censorship and revisionism. He also discusses how the organisation is working to rebuild its credibility after criticism over its failure to condemn the war in Gaza in 2023. The award-winning author’s fourth novel, “Someone Like Us”, has just been published in France, and Mengestu tells us about the personal elements he has woven into this story of an Ethiopian-American grappling with his identity. We also discuss the immigrant experience in the United States and why notions of “us” and “them” remain such divisive forces in public debate.
articles.content:
Novelist Dinaw Mengestu on the battle for freedom of expression

Culture

12:30 min Share

Play (12:30 min)

From the show

Reading time 1 min

As he takes on the presidency of PEN America, Dinaw Mengestu explains why defending a plurality of voices is crucial at a time of censorship and revisionism. He also discusses how the organisation is working to rebuild its credibility after criticism over its failure to condemn the war in Gaza in 2023. The award-winning author’s fourth novel, “Someone Like Us”, has just been published in France, and Mengestu tells us about the personal elements he has woven into this story of an Ethiopian-American grappling with his identity. We also discuss the immigrant experience in the United States and why notions of “us” and “them” remain such divisive forces in public debate.
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Dinaw Mengestu's leadership in PEN America advocating for freedom of expression, a fundamental human right, especially relevant amidst censorship and political conflict. It addresses the importance of defending diverse voices and rebuilding organizational credibility, which has broad societal significance. The focus on immigrant identity and public discourse adds depth to the human rights theme.
articles.personality_title: Dinaw Mengestu leads PEN America to defend free expression and diverse voices
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Dinaw Mengestu, an award-winning novelist, has become the new president of PEN America, an organization that supports freedom of expression and protects writers around the world. Recently, PEN America faced criticism for not speaking out against the war in Gaza in 2023, which affected its reputation.

**What happened** – As president, Mengestu is working to restore trust in PEN America by emphasizing the importance of defending many different voices, especially when censorship and political conflicts threaten free speech. At the same time, he published his fourth novel, “Someone Like Us,” which tells the story of an Ethiopian-American dealing with questions about identity.

**Impact** – Mengestu’s leadership is important because it highlights the need to protect freedom of expression during difficult times. By focusing on diverse voices, PEN America aims to support writers and ideas that might otherwise be silenced. Mengestu’s novel also brings attention to the immigrant experience in the United States, showing how ideas about “us” and “them” can divide people.

**What’s next step** – PEN America plans to continue its work defending free speech and rebuilding its reputation. Mengestu’s role may help the organization better respond to future challenges and encourage more open, respectful conversations about difficult issues.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dinaw Mengestu’s leadership at PEN America strengthens the defense of free expression and highlights the value of diverse voices in today’s world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Dinaw Mengestu dirige PEN America pour défendre la liberté d’expression et la diversité des voix
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dinaw Mengestu, romancier récompensé, est devenu le nouveau président de PEN America, une organisation qui soutient la liberté d’expression et protège les écrivains dans le monde. Récemment, PEN America a été critiquée pour ne pas avoir condamné la guerre à Gaza en 2023, ce qui a affecté sa réputation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En tant que président, Mengestu travaille à restaurer la confiance envers PEN America en soulignant l’importance de défendre de nombreuses voix différentes, surtout lorsque la censure et les conflits politiques menacent la liberté d’expression. En même temps, il a publié son quatrième roman, « Someone Like Us », qui raconte l’histoire d’un Éthiopien-Américain confronté à des questions d’identité.

**Impact** – Le leadership de Mengestu est important car il met en lumière la nécessité de protéger la liberté d’expression en période difficile. En se concentrant sur la diversité des voix, PEN America vise à soutenir les écrivains et les idées qui pourraient autrement être réduits au silence. Le roman de Mengestu attire aussi l’attention sur l’expérience des immigrants aux États-Unis, montrant comment les notions de « nous » et « eux » peuvent diviser les gens.

**Prochaine étape** – PEN America prévoit de poursuivre son travail de défense de la liberté d’expression et de reconstruction de sa réputation. Le rôle de Mengestu pourrait aider l’organisation à mieux répondre aux défis futurs et encourager des conversations plus ouvertes et respectueuses sur des sujets difficiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le leadership de Dinaw Mengestu à PEN America renforce la défense de la liberté d’expression et souligne la valeur des voix diverses dans le monde d’aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: Dinaw Mengestu lidera PEN America para defender la libertad de expresión y la diversidad de voces
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Dinaw Mengestu, un novelista premiado, se ha convertido en el nuevo presidente de PEN America, una organización que apoya la libertad de expresión y protege a los escritores en todo el mundo. Recientemente, PEN America fue criticada por no condenar la guerra en Gaza en 2023, lo que afectó su reputación.

**Qué pasó** – Como presidente, Mengestu está trabajando para restaurar la confianza en PEN America destacando la importancia de defender muchas voces diferentes, especialmente cuando la censura y los conflictos políticos amenazan la libertad de expresión. Al mismo tiempo, publicó su cuarta novela, “Someone Like Us”, que cuenta la historia de un etíope-estadounidense que enfrenta preguntas sobre su identidad.

**Impacto** – El liderazgo de Mengestu es importante porque muestra la necesidad de proteger la libertad de expresión en tiempos difíciles. Al enfocarse en voces diversas, PEN America busca apoyar a escritores e ideas que podrían ser silenciadas. La novela de Mengestu también pone atención a la experiencia de los inmigrantes en Estados Unidos y cómo las ideas de “nosotros” y “ellos” pueden dividir a las personas.

**Próximo paso** – PEN America planea continuar su trabajo defendiendo la libertad de expresión y recuperando su reputación. El papel de Mengestu podría ayudar a la organización a responder mejor a futuros desafíos y fomentar conversaciones más abiertas y respetuosas sobre temas difíciles.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El liderazgo de Dinaw Mengestu en PEN America fortalece la defensa de la libertad de expresión y resalta el valor de las voces diversas en el mundo actual.
